front: Building: transitional cathedral in Christchurch, New Zealand, built after the 2011 earthquake; eight shipping containers form the walls; triangular polycarbonate roof
Building: cardboard tubes stacked on top of shipping containers for a New Zealand Anglican cathedral; paper tube studio used in design was incorporated into the final building
Building: art museum in Metz, France — roof of undulating hexagonal glue-laminated timber inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat found in Paris
Building: located in Metz, the most populous city in the Lorraine region of France, at the confluence of the Moselle and Grand Seille rivers
Building: Metz art museum with a glass elevator inspired by a gondola, from which visitors 'ski' down to the galleries
Building: Paper Dome / Takatori Catholic Church in Kobe — built with paper tubes after the 1995 Great Hanshin earthquake; later moved to Taomi Village, Taiwan in 1999
Building: Japanese Pavilion at Expo 2000 in Hanover — collaborated with Frei Otto; roof made entirely of cardboard tubes shaped like a honeycomb
Architect: developed the Paper Partition System for temporary housing for earthquake disaster victims
Architect: substituted aluminum rods with cardboard tubes for refugee camps in Byumba, Rwanda
Architect: won the 2014 Pritzker Prize; the prize was established in 1979 by a co-founder of the Hyatt hotel chain
Architect: 2013 Pritzker winner Toyo Ito designed the Sendai Mediatheque; this Japanese architect won the following year
Architect: designed transparent glass toilets for the 2020 Tokyo Olympics whose walls turn opaque when occupied ('smart glass')
Architect: designed the Aspen Museum of Art — his first US museum; the previous facility was housed in a converted hydroelectric plant
Architect: designed the Nomadic Museum for photographer Gregory Colbert's Ashes and Snow exhibition
Architect: some structures anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates; summer house on Lake Yamanaka was among the first buildings to use paper tubes structurally
